story:
He came home late, breath full of onions, garlic, and something smoky-sweet that always warned me to tread carefully. When I asked—again—for a simple text so I’d know whether to take Max out and when to start dinner, and added, “And who is Tinsley—the one you’re going to ask for photos later, the one you’ll tell the hidden money is safe and that you two will fly to Cabo?” he erupted. He accused me of demanding a lifestyle he worked to fund, of policing him with location sharing, of not trusting him. He punched the wall, another divot among many, and a wedding photo toppled. I wondered what “lifestyle” he meant, and who Tinsley was; he drove the new Range Rover, while my professor’s salary covered his golf club dues and classes.

I tried to keep my voice calm. He shifted from shouting to a quiet, mean calm, sneering that I thought I was smarter because I had a PhD. I snapped and mentioned his academic probation in college. His hand cracked across my face. The room dimmed and then came back into focus. My blood dotted our white sofa—white like everything else he insisted on. He dropped to his knees, apologizing, pressing frozen peas to my lip, begging forgiveness. I gave the robotic “I forgive you” he wanted and went upstairs to change.

In the bathroom light, my lip had already doubled in size, a bruise bleeding outward. I heard him stomp upstairs a few minutes later, muttering about checking something. Through the cracked bathroom door, I watched him sit at his desk, tap open the laptop, scroll through something quickly, then head back down, leaving it open and glowing. He hadn’t even bothered to close the lid—the carelessness of a man who’d never once imagined I’d look. Below, he talked into AirPods and splashed bourbon while dumping pasta into boiling water. His distraction was my chance.

I crept to the desk. His email overflowed with messages from Ashley Madison, Hinge, Tinder, Match—going back years, even before our wedding. Dates with Vanessa at a wine bar, with Emma at an absinthe bar. I clicked to a different tab and froze. A bank account I didn’t recognize, already open in his browser. A strip club charge that afternoon. A balance of $145,000. While my paycheck covered his toys and classes, he’d been hiding money for clubs and flings. I set an ACH transfer of $140,000 to the personal “oh no” account my dad once told me to keep, timed for midnight. When it asked for the password, I tried my name and birthdate. It worked. I changed it to something he’d never guess, not because I thought it would protect the money forever, but because it might buy me a few hours before he understood what I’d done.

He called up that dinner was ready; I told him I’d lie down. In the dim, I replayed how we’d gotten here: senior year bar crawl; him, tall and beautiful; me, smitten; a flashy eight-month engagement. Then the red flags I’d minimized because he was charming—bar fights, dead phones, a night in jail with excuses that sounded good in the moment. For a breath I wondered if any of it was my fault, and then I refused to gaslight myself like he had.

Later, I crept downstairs. He didn’t see me. Into his AirPods, he murmured to Tinsley, asking for photos, promising that the hidden money was safe, that they’d fly to Cabo. The truth settled cold and clear: the cheating, the lies, the slap, and now a plan to discard me after he’d emptied what he could and left me to clean up the wreckage. His new white leather golf bag gleamed in the corner. I pulled the driver free, the club he loved more than our vows, and stepped close.

I didn’t swing for revenge or triumph. I swung because, in that instant, I believed if I let him finish the call and turn around, I would lose my chance to get out safely. 

I bent over and looked at him. It really was all his fault.
